original_abstract:
Science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) education is often framed as a catalyst for global dominance and a universal solution to economic volatility, environmental crises, and food insecurity. However, significant variations in regional and national agendas reveal a stark divide in policy formulation and implementation between the Global North and Global South. This study examines the underlying drivers of STEM education within these two geo-economic blocs to better understand the contextual factors influencing their practices. Such a focused comparison is essential given the rising demand for international collaboration on issues like the Sustainable Development Goals and global health crises. 

Adopting a theoretical framework rooted in literature review and ideology critique, this analysis identifies divergent motivations. In the Global North, STEM education is primarily propelled by a neoliberal agenda, characterized by ambitions of political hegemony, a desire to mitigate economic downturns, and an emerging interest in extraterrestrial colonization. Conversely, the Global South focuses on massification anchored in equity, as nations strive to redress colonial-era imbalances. While Global South countries frequently subscribe to international mandates, their efforts are often hampered by financial constraints and a persistent brain drain, leading to stagnation at the vocational implementation stage. 

Furthermore, "convenient partnerships" have emerged, wherein Global North nations leverage the geographical and natural resource advantages of the Global South to advance extraterrestrial, biomedical, and indigenous resource research. To move toward more equitable global practices, the Global North must actively redistribute the power dynamics it holds within the STEM landscape. We recommend that the Global South prioritize the vocationalization of STEM by integrating it with the humanities, while advocating for collaborative frameworks that ensure mutual benefit rather than exploitation.
humanized_abstract:
The promotion of science, technology, engineering and mathematics (STEM) education is ubiquitous worldwide, hailed as the panacea to achieve global leadership in today’s highly competitive world, to counteract economic fluctuations, environmental degradation and food shortages. However, this common mantra on STEM hides a diverse landscape of intentions and actions at the national level, which contrast greatly between the Global North and the Global South. Through an in-depth analysis of the underlying drivers that motivate policy-making at the national level on STEM, this paper aims to shed light on the specific and varied context shaping each country’s actions in each of the two main economic blocks of the world. It is high time for such a comparison as there is indeed an increasing need for global cooperation to achieve the United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goals and respond to global health emergencies.

Drawing on literature review and ideology critique as theoretical frameworks for understanding different motivations behind the promotion of STEM education and the massification of education in the Global South and North. Motivations for the promotion of science, technology, engineering and mathematics (STEM) education differ in the Global South and North. In the North, the promotion of STEM is carried out above all in the name of neoliberalism – with political, economic and recently also spatial motivations in view (colonizing Mars). In the South, the policy of massification with an emphasis on equity is connected with the aim of redressing the imbalances and inequalities that arose during the colonial era. While southern countries may subscribe to international goals and recommendations, these are often not translated into practice, not least on account of the countries’ limited economic resources and on-going brain drain – resulting in stagnation at the level of vocational training.

There are convenient partnerships which are carried out by using resources of the Global South such as geographical advantages and natural resources in order to develop researches in the area of space exploration, biomedical and indigenous resource research. In order to develop a more just and equal practice at the world wide scale, the Global North must make a change within the power structure of the STEM fields. Recommendation: For the Global South it is necessary to develop vocational STEM education by connecting it to the humanities as well as to advocate for a partnership model that is fair for all parties involved.
